title: Capacity Forecasting Case Overview
subtitle: How raw telemetry becomes a practical capacity decision

nodes.label: Business Demand
nodes.definition: The expected service usage driven by customer and internal demand.
nodes.whyItMatters: Capacity planning starts with expected demand, not infrastructure guesses.
nodes.safeSentence: Business demand frames how much capacity we need and when we need it.

nodes.label: Application
nodes.definition: The service or workload whose performance and stability must be protected.
nodes.whyItMatters: Capacity decisions are meaningful only when mapped to specific applications.
nodes.safeSentence: We forecast capacity at the application level so actions stay accountable.

nodes.label: Telemetry
nodes.definition: Operational measurements such as CPU, memory, throughput, and latency over time.
nodes.whyItMatters: Telemetry is the evidence base for baseline and forecast calculations.
nodes.safeSentence: Reliable telemetry turns capacity planning into evidence-based decision support.

nodes.label: Baseline
nodes.definition: A stable reference pattern of recent usage behavior.
nodes.whyItMatters: A baseline gives context for growth and risk movement.
nodes.safeSentence: Baseline behavior helps us separate normal variation from trend change.

nodes.label: Forecast
nodes.definition: A forward estimate of expected resource usage over a defined horizon.
nodes.whyItMatters: Forecasts indicate whether current capacity can sustain expected demand.
nodes.safeSentence: The forecast is decision support, not certainty; it guides early action.

nodes.label: Threshold
nodes.definition: A predefined utilization limit that signals elevated service risk.
nodes.whyItMatters: Thresholds convert raw values into actionable alerts.
nodes.safeSentence: We compare forecasted usage to thresholds to trigger proportionate actions.

nodes.label: Headroom
nodes.definition: Remaining safe capacity between expected usage and practical limits.
nodes.whyItMatters: Headroom explains urgency and action timing.
nodes.safeSentence: Headroom tells us how much buffer remains before service risk rises.

nodes.label: Risk Band
nodes.definition: A low, medium, or high category assigned from forecast and headroom signals.
nodes.whyItMatters: Risk bands create a shared language for prioritization.
nodes.safeSentence: Risk bands keep escalation and response decisions consistent.

nodes.label: Dashboard
nodes.definition: A summarized operational view showing trend, forecast, and risk indicators.
nodes.whyItMatters: Decision makers need a common view of current and projected state.
nodes.safeSentence: The dashboard aligns stakeholders on facts before action is chosen.

nodes.label: Decision
nodes.definition: The agreed operational response based on forecast evidence and business impact.
nodes.whyItMatters: Capacity work is complete only when it results in a clear decision.
nodes.safeSentence: Forecasting supports decisions; governance ensures they are owned and executed.

nodes.label: Owner
nodes.definition: The accountable application or infrastructure lead responsible for execution.
nodes.whyItMatters: Named ownership prevents decision drift and delays.
nodes.safeSentence: A clear owner converts capacity recommendations into operational follow-through.

nodes.label: Action Plan
nodes.definition: A dated set of actions, approvals, and checkpoints tied to risk level.
nodes.whyItMatters: A plan translates decision intent into trackable operational work.
nodes.safeSentence: The action plan defines what will happen, who will do it, and by when.

paths.label: Telemetry to Decision
paths.description: Follow the practical path from demand and telemetry through forecast, risk, and operational action.
paths.rehearsalPrompt: Explain how telemetry evidence becomes a governed capacity action plan.
